quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Vector"?

options.text: s = rθ; the arc length swept by a point at radius r when the object rotates through angle θ (in radians)
A quantity with both magnitude and direction, represented as an arrow; examples include displacement, velocity, force, and momentum
F_net = dp/dt; the net force equals the time rate of change of momentum
F_ext,net = M_total × a_cm; the center of mass accelerates as if all mass and net external force were concentrated there

quiz_questions.explanation: "Vector" is defined as: A quantity with both magnitude and direction, represented as an arrow; examples include displacement, velocity, force, and momentum. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Component decomposition"?

options.text: A mass m on a spring with constant k oscillates with ω = √(k/m) and T = 2π√(m/k)
sinθ ≈ θ (in radians) for small angles; this linearizes the restoring torque and produces SHM
Breaking a vector into perpendicular components: Ax = Acosθ, Ay = Asinθ where θ is measured from the positive x-axis
U = -GMm/r; defined as zero at infinite separation; always negative for bound systems

quiz_questions.explanation: "Component decomposition" is defined as: Breaking a vector into perpendicular components: Ax = Acosθ, Ay = Asinθ where θ is measured from the positive x-axis. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Dot product"?

options.text: F_ext = dp/dt applied to rockets: thrust = v_exhaust × |dm/dt|, where mass decreases as fuel is expelled
U = ½kx²; the energy stored in a spring displaced x from equilibrium
In collisions, impulsive forces are so large and brief that other forces (gravity, friction) are negligible during the collision
A·B = ABcosθ = AxBx + AyBy; a scalar measuring the projection of one vector onto another; used in calculating work

quiz_questions.explanation: "Dot product" is defined as: A·B = ABcosθ = AxBx + AyBy; a scalar measuring the projection of one vector onto another; used in calculating work. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Cross product"?

options.text: A uniform spherical shell attracts external masses as if all its mass were at the center; inside the shell, gravitational force from the shell is zero
A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force
The energy required to completely separate a system; equals |E_total| for a gravitationally bound system
A force whose work is path-independent; equivalently, the work around any closed path is zero; examples: gravity, spring force

quiz_questions.explanation: "Cross product" is defined as: A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following best defines "Unit vector"?

options.text: When two waves overlap, the resultant displacement is the sum of individual displacements; leads to interference and standing waves
Circular motion with changing speed; has both centripetal (radial) and tangential acceleration components
A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively
A mass on a string tracing a horizontal circle; T sinθ provides centripetal force while T cosθ balances gravity

quiz_questions.explanation: "Unit vector" is defined as: A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively. The other options describe different concepts.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A quantity with both magnitude and direction, represented as an arrow; examples include displacement, velocity, force, and momentum"?

options.text: Common moments of inertia
Rotational kinematics
Vector
Work-Energy Theorem

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Vector".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "Breaking a vector into perpendicular components: Ax = Acosθ, Ay = Asinθ where θ is measured from the positive x-axis"?

options.text: Component decomposition
Simple pendulum period
Conservation of angular momentum
Superposition

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Component decomposition".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A·B = ABcosθ = AxBx + AyBy; a scalar measuring the projection of one vector onto another; used in calculating work"?

options.text: Work by a spring
Restoring torque
Dot product
Angular momentum (rigid body)

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Dot product".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A×B; a vector perpendicular to both A and B with magnitude ABsinθ; used in calculating torque and magnetic force"?

options.text: Conservation of angular momentum
Hooke's Law
Recoil
Cross product

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Cross product".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which term is described by: "A dimensionless vector of magnitude 1 indicating direction; î, ĵ, k̂ denote the x, y, z directions respectively"?

options.text: Velocity and acceleration in SHM
Elastic collision
Linear momentum
Unit vector

quiz_questions.explanation: This description refers to "Unit vector".

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following statements about Vectors and Components is accurate?

options.text: In AP Physics C, the calculus formulation F = dp/dt can be applied to systems with time-varying forces, including rocket propulsion where mass changes continuously.
Vectors and Components provide the mathematical language for describing physical quantities with both magnitude and direction.
Pendulums are oscillating systems where gravity provides the restoring torque.
Angular Kinematics describes rotational motion using angular position θ(t), angular velocity ω(t) = dθ/dt, and angular acceleration α(t) = dω/dt.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Vectors and Components as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which statement correctly describes an aspect of Vectors and Components?

options.text: The physical (compound) pendulum accounts for distributed mass: T = 2π√(I/(mgd)), where I is the moment of inertia about the pivot and d is the distance from pivot to center of mass.
Conservation of Energy states that in an isolated system, total mechanical energy (KE + PE) remains constant when only conservative forces do work: KE₁ + PE₁ = KE₂ + PE₂.
Any vector can be decomposed into perpendicular components (typically x and y), enabling independent analysis of motion and forces in each direction.
A force is conservative if the work it does depends only on initial and final positions, not the path.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Vectors and Components as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which is true specifically about Vectors and Components?

options.text: Work and Kinetic Energy defines work rigorously using calculus: W = ∫F·dr, the line integral of force along the displacement path.
Work is defined as W = ∫F·dr (the dot product integral of force along the displacement path), and the Work-Energy Theorem states W_net = ΔKE = ½mv² - ½mv₀².
In perfectly inelastic collisions, the objects stick together and maximum KE is lost (converted to thermal/sound energy).
Vector addition, subtraction, dot product (A·B = ABcosθ), and cross product (A×B, magnitude = ABsinθ) are essential operations throughout mechanics.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Vectors and Components as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best explains a key idea of Vectors and Components?

options.text: In AP Physics C, the calculus formulation F = dp/dt can be applied to systems with time-varying forces, including rocket propulsion where mass changes continuously.
Orbital Motion applies Newton's Second Law with gravitational force to derive orbital parameters.
Free-body diagrams identify all forces acting on an object, enabling systematic application of F = ma in each coordinate direction.
Vectors and Components provide the mathematical language for describing physical quantities with both magnitude and direction.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Vectors and Components as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which accurately reflects the content on Vectors and Components?

options.text: The simple pendulum (point mass on a massless string) has period T = 2π√(L/g) for small angles, independent of mass and amplitude.
Pendulums are oscillating systems where gravity provides the restoring torque.
Conservation of angular momentum explains phenomena from spinning ice skaters to the formation of galaxies.
Any vector can be decomposed into perpendicular components (typically x and y), enabling independent analysis of motion and forces in each direction.

quiz_questions.explanation: The correct statement describes Vectors and Components as covered in this lesson.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which of the following is a real-world application of Vectors and Components?

options.text: Navigation and GPS systems using vector addition to compute resultant displacement from multiple legs of a journey
Door handle placement: handles are at the edge (maximum r) to maximize torque for a given applied force, making doors easy to open
CD/DVD drive technology computing read speeds at different radii using ω = v/r to maintain constant linear velocity as the read head moves
Roller coaster design using potential energy diagrams to verify sufficient kinetic energy at all points along the track without external power input

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Vectors and Components.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which example demonstrates the practical relevance of Vectors and Components?

options.text: Analyzing asteroid capture: determining whether an approaching asteroid's total energy is negative enough to become bound to a planet's gravitational field
Structural engineering resolving forces on bridge cables into horizontal and vertical components to analyze tension and compression in each member
Helicopter tail rotor design: compensating for the angular momentum of the main rotor to prevent the helicopter body from spinning in the opposite direction
Vehicle accident reconstruction using partially inelastic collision models and coefficient of restitution to determine pre-collision speeds

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Vectors and Components.

quiz_questions.question_text: How is Vectors and Components applied in real-world contexts?

options.text: Elevator engineering calculating cable tension and motor force requirements for different loading conditions using F = ma for the elevator-passenger system
Roller coaster design using potential energy diagrams to verify sufficient kinetic energy at all points along the track without external power input
Calculating the mass of Earth from the known value of g at the surface and Earth's radius: M = gR²/G
Computer graphics and game physics engines using dot and cross products for collision detection, lighting calculations, and surface normal computations

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Vectors and Components.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which scenario illustrates the principles of Vectors and Components?

options.text: Navigation and GPS systems using vector addition to compute resultant displacement from multiple legs of a journey
Musical instrument design: string tension and density determine wave speed and thus the fundamental frequency of vibrating strings
Firearms forensics using conservation of momentum to calculate bullet speed from the recoil velocity of a ballistic pendulum
Wind turbine design optimizing blade angular velocity to maximize energy capture while maintaining safe centripetal acceleration at blade tips

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Vectors and Components.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which best shows Vectors and Components in practice?

options.text: Neutron moderation in nuclear reactors: elastic collisions between neutrons and hydrogen atoms (equal mass) transfer maximum kinetic energy in a single collision
Structural engineering resolving forces on bridge cables into horizontal and vertical components to analyze tension and compression in each member
Forensic physics reconstructing car accident speeds from skid mark lengths using W_friction = ΔKE to calculate the initial velocity
Bridge engineering calculating torques about supports to determine forces on beams and ensure structural equilibrium

quiz_questions.explanation: This is a real-world application of Vectors and Components.
